turns.content: Hey, I just implemented a basic hot-reload system using unsafe ptr::read and raw pointers to swap out modules at runtime. It works but feels incredibly slow because of all the memory copying involved.

That's a classic trap for Rust developers trying to avoid the borrow checker without understanding the underlying OS behavior. The issue isn't really about swapping logic at runtime; it's that you're treating your runtime as if it were just memory, ignoring how actual dynamic library loading works with FFI and shared libraries.

I know I should probably use something like the hot-lib-reloader crate or a custom loader written in C for the heavy lifting. But I'm stuck because my current approach doesn't support swapping logic at runtime efficiently, and I can't figure out how to actually replace modules without restarting the process.

You're right that you need something more sophisticated than raw pointer manipulation. The real solution involves using a proper dynamic library loading mechanism where your application doesn't own the code directly but instead delegates execution to a separate process or library context.
That sounds complicated though. I need something that feels like it's doing hot-reload natively without me writing all this boilerplate around loading and unloading shared objects manually.

The key insight is that you don't actually need to implement the dynamic library loading yourself if you use a framework designed for dev-tools. Most modern Rust toolchains have built-in support through crates like cargo-watch or specialized hot-reload libraries that handle the complex file watching and binary reconstruction internally.
Wait, so I should just use those existing tools instead of building my own unsafe pointer swapping logic? That would save me a lot of debugging time on memory corruption issues.

Exactly. The frustration you're feeling comes from trying to solve an advanced problem with primitive techniques. Those crates handle the complex parts like watching for file changes and reconstructing binaries at runtime, letting you focus on your actual application logic.
Makes sense. So I stop worrying about manual memory management and just plug into the existing ecosystem? What's the best way to integrate this into my current dev workflow?

You can add those dependencies to your Cargo.toml and configure them in your config files. They'll automatically detect changes, rebuild the necessary components using safe patterns, and restart only what needs restarting while keeping everything else running.
